title: Mechanisms of mindfulness: emotion regulation following a focused breathing induction
headline: Mechanisms of mindfulness: emotion regulation following a focused breathing induction
summary: A randomized controlled trial of breath-and-attention meditation examined emotional wellbeing. The current study investigated whether a 15 min recorded focused breathing induction in a normal, primarily undergraduate population would decrease the intensity and negativity of emotional responses to affectively valenced picture slides and increase willingness to remain in contact with aversive picture slides.
keyFindings: The current study investigated whether a 15 min recorded focused breathing induction in a normal, primarily undergraduate population would decrease the intensity and negativity of emotional responses to affectively valenced picture slides and increase willingness to remain in contact with aversive picture slides.
The effects of the focused breathing induction were compared with the effects of 15 min recorded inductions of unfocused attention and worrying.
limitations: Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (breath-and-attention meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared breath-regulation and directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Mechanisms of mindfulness: emotion regulation following a focused breathing induction
aiSnippet: RCT, Behaviour research and therapy (2006). The current study investigated whether a 15 min recorded focused breathing induction in a normal, primarily undergraduate population would decrease the intensity and negativity of emotional responses to affectively valenced picture slides and increase willingness to remain in contact with aversive picture slides. The effects of the focused breathing induction were compared with the effects of 15 min recorded inductions of unfocused attention and worrying.
whyItMatters: MoM shares breath regulation and directed attention in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
